title: Floating Time Crystals That Break a Science Rule
summary: Scientists have made a special kind of matter called a time crystal. It is made of tiny beads that float in the air using sound waves. These beads move back and forth in a steady pattern, like ticking. In this new time crystal, the beads do not follow a famous science rule by Isaac Newton. The rule says that when two things push each other, they push back equally. But here, bigger beads push smaller beads more than the smaller ones push back. This makes their movement uneven. The experiment was done by scientists at New York University. They used a small device that you can hold in your hand. Time crystals might help in future technologies like computers. They could also teach us about how our bodies keep time, like when we sleep and wake up.

keywords.explanation: a special kind of matter that moves in repeating cycles
invisible waves that can make things float or move
tiny round objects used in the experiment
a science rule that says forces come in equal pairs
to stay in the air without falling
people who study and do experiments to learn new things

How do the beads float in the air?
Which science rule do the beads break?
What might time crystals help with in the future?
What is one thing time crystals could teach us about?

background_read: Time crystals are a new type of matter that scientists discovered about ten years ago. They are like tiny clocks because their parts move in regular patterns. Isaac Newton was a famous scientist who made rules about how things move, like his Third Law that says every push has an equal push back. In this article, scientists use sound to make beads float and create a time crystal that doesn't follow this rule. This could lead to better technology and help us understand natural rhythms in life.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists at New York University, including Professor David Grier and students Mia Morrell and Leela Elliott
WHAT: They created a floating time crystal using sound waves and tiny beads that breaks Newton's Third Law
WHERE: In a laboratory at New York University
WHY: To explore new possibilities for technology and understand biological timing systems like circadian rhythms

perspectives.description: This shows how scientists can create new forms of matter that challenge old rules, opening doors for future inventions.
